notes: World Cities Culture Forum study — most of any city in the world measured, ahead of Hong Kong's second-place 22. Study dates to 2015, still cited in 2026 sources, not a fresh recount

value_raw: 370-hectare/914-acre park complex sitting directly in Palermo, plus the free Jardín Botánico
notes: Genuinely substantial urban nature access. City average green space 6.3 sqm/person, ranging 0.2 to 22.9 by district — Palermo sits toward the high end
SpeedGeo/Ookla-sourced, not a vendor claim. Movistar's own network averages 219.4/205.7 Mbps over the most recent 12 months, at 18ms latency

value_raw: A genuine world city — ~15 million metro, deepest digital-nomad/remote-worker infrastructure of anywhere in Argentina
notes: Palermo Soho is the actual remote-work cluster; Recoleta the formal/older-money "settled expat/retiree" counterpart; Belgrano the quieter, more residential, still-safe third option

value_raw: Mid-70s°F/mid-20s°C summer, low-50s°F/11-12°C winter
notes: Humid subtropical (Köppen Cfa) — hot sticky summers (70-90% humidity, frequent thunderstorms), cool grey damp winters (90%+ humidity), no genuine dry season

Scored biome-neutrally per this project's own climate-neutrality convention's confirmed reading: humid subtropical, no dry season, a genuine year-round comfort factor independent of any preference question, not a shortfall against Guatemala's mildness
Real dense infrastructure comparable to Medellín's depth-leader status, held below 5 by a genuine expat-bubble/dollar-peso friction, weighed as real rather than left narrative-only
Sources converge on $1,200-2,500/month single expat in Palermo; scored on the same "workable at the bottom of the range isn't comfortable" logic used elsewhere

value_raw: AreaTres, Urban Station, WeWork (all Palermo locations); The Shelter (Australian-owned nomad hub, Palermo); La Maquinita runs an active Slack alongside its physical space
notes: Palermo Soho described repeatedly as "a true haven for digital nomads"

value_raw: 200 (hot-desk) / 1500-4500 (private office, small team)
notes: Private-office figure relevant only if a small local team is ever in play, not solo remote work

value_raw: 'Technology expenses 50% higher than the US' and 'lack of functional banking' named as real, current friction specific to remote workers
notes: Surfaced by the same negative-account hunt as the broadband finding above

value_raw: Argentines earning pesos and foreigners earning dollars are functionally in two different housing markets in Palermo/Recoleta/Belgrano
notes: Buenos Aires Times documented case: a professional-class peso-earning couple lost their home to a price hike, now living with a parent

value_raw: Reddit/BAExpats threads describe surface-level connections requiring real effort to build local friendships; BAExpats' own forum carries an internal critique of 'pseudo digital nomads...that at age 25 have lost themselves'
notes: Surfaced by the dedicated negative-account hunt Antigua's bias audit found missing — real friction, not just cost-line
Lands inside Numbeo's own $1,407-1,632 citywide range — genuine site-to-site agreement. Direct fetch blocked (403); figures corroborated across two independent search passes
A week's groceries ~$30-50
Deepest remote-income ecosystem researched, but held a point below a territorial-tax location for the real worldwide-income/Bienes Personales/no-US-treaty drag

fact_label: INDEC Canasta Básica Total (poverty-line floor, single adult)
notes: A bare, government-defined non-poverty floor, not a comfortable-lifestyle budget — useful only as a sanity-check floor. Family-of-four figure (ARS 1,498,741, ~$990-1,000) independently confirms the peso-vs-dollar divide named from anecdote elsewhere in this file

value_raw: +2.1 (May 2026 MoM); +33.2 (YoY); +14.7 (Jan-May 2026 accumulated)
notes: INDEC (Instituto Nacional de Estadística y Censos) — Argentina's official statistics agency. A real deceleration trend (Jan +2.9% → May +2.1%), not flat
Confirmed non-vendor broadband data; held at 4 not 5 for real, current digital-nomad-specific tech/banking friction

Open ownership, no restricted zone for CABA property; Law 26.737's repeal is itself frozen by judicial order (moot for city property either way). Held below 5 for the CDI trip-point and no residency conferred by purchase

value_raw: A simplified flat-tax regime for software developers, biotech/nanotech professionals, and other knowledge-economy freelancers working for foreign clients — working through Congress
notes: Unsettled policy, not yet in force
Real park nature (Bosques de Palermo), weak water-adjacency (Río de la Plata not swimmable) — a new-research row, no prior score existed
Palermo, Belgrano, Núñez, Colegiales specifically — worth knowing before assuming a per-m² figure applies uniformly regardless of building age

value_raw: [GAP]
notes: A genuinely open question whether either offers a more realistic entry point than Buenos Aires city

value_raw: Companies owned/operated by AI agents, able to sign contracts and hold assets without a human principal — announced June 3, 2026
notes: Genuinely unusual, genuinely current — worth ongoing watch, not settled policy, announcements don't always survive contact with Congress

value_raw: 736.38 (city-centre) / 511.00 (outside-centre)
notes: numbeo.com, "Last update: 6 July 2026," 1,374 entries/162 contributors past 12 months. Single-person costs excl. rent: $895.70/month. This is a citywide figure (includes San Telmo, Once, Congreso, Balvanera) — a measurement-scope difference from this file's Palermo-specific figures, not a real disagreement
Some sources cite a narrower $1,000-1,200 band for un-fancy units. Furnished units carry a 20-40% premium over unfurnished
$1,500-2,000/month the more commonly cited "comfortable, not luxurious" figure. Recoleta specifically would likely push above this range

Three real, dated events pulling in different directions (currency-controls lift favorable; Decree 366/2025 immigration tightening unfavorable; Oct 2026 election stabilizing) — nets to 3, a structurally worse base rate than a typical candidate with a real near-term fiscal stress test still ahead, not an active crisis
OSDE and Swiss Medical the two names that recur constantly — buys access to the internationally-oriented private hospital network

value_raw: ~2725 average, 2675 median (2-3 ambiente); 3200-3800 (Avenida Cabildo/near top schools); 2400-2600 (Belgrano Norte/Bajo Belgrano)
2800–3900 (one source); 3300–3400 (a second, narrower figure)
notes: thelatinvestor.com, propertyinbuenosaires.com. Typical 1-3 room modern-building unit: $150,000-$300,000

value_raw: 3000–7000 (citywide-comparison figure); 2500–3500 (typical 2-3 ambiente units); 3800–4500 (renovated heritage units, prime blocks)
notes: Typical unit: $120,000-$500,000. Wide range reflects buyers paying for location/walkability/architecture, per the source's own framing
Citywide ~4%, down from near-zero in 2023 but still a landlord's market; Palermo/Belgrano specifically 2-3%, tighter than the city average

fact_label: Río de la Plata — water-adjacency quality
value_raw: Wide, brown, silt-heavy estuary — not swimmable or recreational; no real beach culture inside CABA itself
notes: The weak half of an otherwise real park-nature strength

fact_label: Room for others / group viability — scorecard score
notes: No dedicated group-viability pass has been run; a fresh search found no Argentina-specific precedent for a small foreign group settling (the "Andinia Plan" conspiracy theory concerns rumored large-scale Patagonian land acquisition, doesn't transfer). Genuinely unknown, not genuinely good
No volcanic/seismic risk (unlike Guatemala) but real traffic/commute-time index and recurring large-scale protests/strikes as a real, current feature of daily life
Real neighborhood-specific homicide data; Numbeo's citywide Safety Index (37.01) is a real counter-signal that can't override neighborhood-specific data but is worth holding the score against
Landlords in premium neighborhoods quote and expect USD payment, not depreciating pesos, since capital-controls lift

value_raw: 6 lines, 90 stations, 56.7km total — genuinely smaller than younger systems in Mexico City, Santiago, Medellín; almost no new line opened 1944-2007
notes: An honest non-finding from the positive-lever hunt: historic, useful, centrally located, but not a standout by current-day coverage. "Oldest in the region" doesn't imply "best"

value_raw: Ranked among the world's best opera houses for acoustics — National Geographic top-10; Leo Beranek's acoustics-expert survey placing it best in the world for opera, second-best for concerts
notes: Real host-city cultural depth — doesn't cancel out the expat-bubble/dollar-peso friction, which is about the foreign-resident experience specifically
$100-150/month for a more generous mid-range setup. Internet $15-80/month depending on speed tier

fact_label: Visa & legal pathway ease — scorecard score
notes: Raised 3→4 on Inversionista's primary-source-verified re-read (no sector restriction, real small-business precedent). Held at 4, not 5: Rentista's real income shortfall and Digital Nomad's dead end both still weigh on the row
Currency stabilization's durability depends on the Central Bank's ability to keep building reserves — not yet a settled, durable equilibrium

fact_label: Argentina-wide dangerous-region equivalent to Colombia's conflict zones
value_raw: [GAP]
notes: Not specifically searched for this pass — a real gap before calling Argentina uniformly safe outside Buenos Aires

value_raw: Yes
notes: Annual wealth tax on worldwide assets above a threshold — a foreign brokerage account or a home back home comes into scope, not just Argentine holdings

fact_label: Boleto de compraventa does not convey ownership
value_raw: Yes
notes: Only the registered escritura pública (public deed) conveys ownership and priority over other claims — described as the most common wrong assumption foreign buyers make

value_raw: Discouraged-but-still-largely-functional as of mid-2026, not yet an active crackdown — a rising-risk gradient, not a hard stop
notes: Decree 366/2025 (May 2025) gave migration authorities explicit authority to reject entry on suspicion a declared purpose doesn't match real intent. BAExpats forum texture: first 1-2 runs typically go through fine, subsequent runs described as "a gamble," more friction for people with prior extensions on record

fact_label: Business closures / job losses since Milei took office
value_raw: 2,000+ businesses closed, ~73,000 jobs lost, concentrated in manufacturing
notes: The backdrop the "currency arbitrage is gone" headline sits on top of, not a separate story

fact_label: CDI (tax ID) requirement to close a purchase
value_raw: Required from AFIP/ARCA — described as the single document that trips up more foreign buyers than any visa or residency issue
notes: People don't learn about it until closing

value_raw: Media-reported possible $500,000+ threshold — separate track, does not modify or replace Article 23(d)'s existing route
notes: DNU 366/2025 plus Decreto 524/2025 (published July 31, 2025). No official figure published as of a January 2026 check — aimed at a much wealthier investor tier

value_raw: 2 continuous years of legal residency with zero interruptions of any kind
notes: Decree 366/2025 eliminated the previous practice of maintaining eligibility through brief trips abroad — a materially stricter rule than "2 years" reads on its face

Genuinely lower than Belize's 10-12% or Crete's 7-10%, though methodology differences make precise cross-country comparison imprecise. Escribano (notary) is mandatory for every transaction — buyer's own independently-chosen escribano is standard fraud-prevention practice

value_raw: Código Civil y Comercial Articles 1983-2036 (enacted 2014, effective 2015) — same underlying shape as Guatemala's/Colombia's proindiviso, Mexico's copropiedad en indiviso, just a different name
notes: Each co-owner holds an undivided share; ending the arrangement without every co-owner's agreement requires a judicial división de condominio (court-supervised partition)

value_raw: Most capital controls (cepo cambiario) lifted April 2025, backed by a $20B IMF program — official/MEP/blue-dollar rates within 2-3% of each other as of early 2026, vs. 100%+ gap as recently as 2023
notes: The blue-dollar/cueva arbitrage is genuinely gone — a real, structural rules-of-the-game change

fact_label: Digital Nomad visa accepts passive income as qualifying proof
value_raw: No
notes: Requires foreign-sourced remote-work income (freelance clients, foreign employer, or foreign-registered online business) — mirrors Colombia's active-income-only framing

value_raw: Transitory (non-resident) visa, explicitly not a residence category
notes: Decree 366/2025 formalized this. Up to 180 days, renewable once (360 days/one cycle total) — does not lead to permanent residence under any circumstances, same trap as Colombia's equivalent

value_raw: Documented case: a peso-earning Argentine professional couple lost their apartment to a dollar-driven price hike; a foreign digital nomad's own on-record 'insane' framing of the dynamic
notes: Buenos Aires Times reporting. The one live thread that does connect to foreigners specifically — individual-level resentment/awareness, not organized action

Fake ownership documents, an unauthorized seller, or pressure to pay through unofficial channels. Most commonly targets remote buyers shopping via online listings, communicating mainly via WhatsApp, relying on the seller's own agent

fact_label: Gentrification backlash — no organized foreigner/nomad-targeted pattern found
value_raw: No evidence of organized protest or explicit anti-foreigner/anti-digital-nomad messaging in Buenos Aires, any neighborhood, any year searched
notes: Reads closest to Oaxaca's quiet-displacement pole, not CDMX's organized marches or Medellín's poster campaigns. Confidence Medium — this pass lacked Spanish-language local press/social media access
Comparable to or better than mid-sized US cities (well below Chicago, Philadelphia, New Orleans). Belgrano specifically ~70% lower crime than the city's southern neighborhoods

value_raw: Tightening, not loosening — Decree 366/2025 cut PR-abandonment window, imposed zero-interruption citizenship clock, reclassified Digital Nomad as non-resident — even as the same government economically courts foreign remote-income earners (Monotributo Tech bill, 'non-human corporation' proposal)
notes: A real, named tension: economically courting this population while administratively tightening the rules around it
Down from extreme highs but still genuinely high by normal-country standards; monthly inflation ticked up for 5 consecutive months as of early 2026

**Corrected 2026-07-08**: previously stated as $30,000-60,000, an aggregator-conflation error. Two independent sources (luxbrumalis.com.ar, June 2025; goldenharbors.com, May 2026) converge on ~$1,000-1,200 — the ARS figure was fixed in 2010 and never updated against ~350-400x currency devaluation since

fact_label: Inversionista real approval gate — discretionary review
value_raw: 'Interés para el país' standard historically 'never defined,' operating with high discretion — likely the real gate, not the nominal dollar figure
notes: An Argentine immigration lawyer's own practice page describes it this way; independently corroborated by the Argentine government's own stated rationale for building a separate 2025 citizenship-by-investment track, calling this existing regime "eroded by discretion, trapped in a bureaucratic maze"

value_raw: No
notes: Confirmed directly against Decreto 616/2010's own text via Infoleg (Argentina's official legislative database), not aggregator paraphrase — the same check that caught Morocco/Taghazout's wrong score. No sector, business-type, or scale restriction appears anywhere in the operative legal language

value_raw: Named approved cases: a French national's textile-manufacturing venture, and food-service/restaurant ('gastronómicos') ventures
notes: Secondhand via a practicing immigration lawyer's own case experience, not a first-person forum post. First lived-account evidence found that a genuinely small, owner-operated business has cleared this approval process

value_raw: Temporary residency via active business investment (Ley 25.871 Art. 23(d), Decreto 616/2010), leading to permanent residency after 3 years
notes: "Whoever contributes their own assets to carry out activities of interest to the country" — a real residency pathway, not a Visitor-equivalent dead end

value_raw: Yes
notes: Same gap Colombia has. FEIE doesn't help passive income at all — Foreign Tax Credit is the primary tool, contingent on Argentine tax being paid first

value_raw: Milei's La Libertad Avanza won 40%+ nationally, 15 of 24 electoral districts — calmed markets, strengthened his governing hand
notes: Political-transition watchlist scan, 2026-07-07. A real, direct signal distinct from and more informative than the separate ~35% approval-rating figure
Administrative fee-and-exit process, not currently criminal. Valid for 10 consecutive days to depart. Decree 366/2025 removed the informal warning-first grace this used to run on
Decree 366/2025 — spending a year outside Argentina after gaining PR now risks losing it, half the previous grace period

fact_label: Petty theft — the actual common risk
value_raw: Phone snatching specifically, called out repeatedly for Palermo Soho around Plaza Serrano
notes: Not violent crime or home invasion — the honest, common risk in the neighborhoods actually in play

value_raw: Comparable to European standards, English-speaking staff at internationally-oriented private hospitals (Hospital Alemán, Hospital Italiano, Hospital Británico) — one of the least ambiguous positives found anywhere in this project
notes: Private insurance (prepaga — OSDE, Swiss Medical) runs ~$50-150/month. Care quality more uneven in Argentina's poorer northern provinces — not relevant to Buenos Aires/Bariloche/Mendoza specifically
Tax authority renamed ARCA (formerly AFIP, as of 2024) — same function

value_raw: No
notes: Ley 25.871 has no "property owner" visa category — Inversionista requires founding/personally managing a business, not triggered by a real-estate purchase itself

value_raw: Ley 13.512, now Civil and Commercial Code Articles 2037-2072 — each unit gets its own separate, individually-titled deed
notes: Standing recommendation: set up Propiedad Horizontal (or subdivide) at time of purchase rather than buying jointly under condominio and sorting titles out later
Complex title issues, inheritance situations, or border-zone approvals can extend this by several months

fact_label: Re-entry ban for misrepresented entry purpose
notes: The sharpest concrete penalty number found. No first-hand BAExpats account found of an ordinary repeated visa-runner (vs. someone using fake documents) actually receiving the full ban rather than a same-day refusal — "graduated risk, severe worst case rarely realized in practice but real"

fact_label: Real economic displacement — La Boca (documented, pre-dates nomad era)
value_raw: 1,100+ residents displaced in La Boca in 2016 alone (96 eviction trials that year); 54.5% of a sampled cohort no longer at their 2000 address by 2008
notes: Driven by state real-estate "Economic District" policy and judicial eviction, not documented as foreigner/digital-nomad specific

fact_label: Real wages (formal sector, Feb 2026 vs. Nov 2023)
notes: Currency stabilization has come with a real, measurable cost to ordinary Argentines' purchasing power

value_raw: No
notes: Ended in stages (US removed March 2016, Australia July 2017, Canada January 2018) — nobody currently pays a reciprocity fee to enter as a tourist

value_raw: Realistic 6-9 month timeline (not the headline 4-6 months); $5,000-7,000 total cost; mandatory private health insurance, minimum $20,000 coverage
notes: Decreto 366/2025 (effective July 2025) stripped temporary residents of public healthcare access entirely, creating the mandatory-insurance requirement. Rests on immigration-consultancy client-experience descriptions, not an identified first-hand applicant account — Medium, not High, confidence

fact_label: Rentista visa accepts passive income as qualifying proof
value_raw: Yes
notes: Foreign-sourced passive income broadly (investments, annuities, rental income) — no sharper "must be structured as a pension" distinction found the way Colombia/Guatemala carry

value_raw: Temporary residency, evolving toward permanent residency
notes: NOT a Visitor-equivalent dead end, unlike Colombia's Rentista — structurally closer to Guatemala's (direct-to-permanent, just via a longer single step)

fact_label: Rentista visa converts to permanent residency
value_raw: Yes
notes: After 2 continuous years on qualifying temporary residency, a Rentista holder can apply for permanent residency

~5x Argentina's minimum wage (ARS 363,000/month). Moved ~45% in about a year (a March 2025 source cited $1,390/month for the same visa) — pegged to minimum wage, expect continued movement, not a fixed dollar figure

value_raw: Yes
notes: Max 15% foreign ownership of agricultural-frontier land per province/municipality; 1,000-hectare cap on individual holdings in core farming zones. A 2023 repeal attempt was blocked by a court injunction restoring the original limits — contested but currently in force. Moot for Buenos Aires city property

value_raw: Residency-based, not territorial; triggers at PR grant or ~12 months physical presence (also see the separate 183-day tourist-time trigger above)
notes: Opposite of Guatemala's territorial system, same structural shape as Colombia's. Worldwide income in scope once triggered

value_raw: One extension, equal to the original stay (90 days → 180 total, two blocks)
notes: argentina.gob.ar official page. Apply within 60 days before expiration. Cost ~ARS 20,000. Late filing (within 30 days after expiration, voluntary) carries a 50% surcharge, still processed — a genuine grace window, not a hard cliff

value_raw: 90 days visa-free (US, EU, Canada, Australia, Japan, NZ, South Korea)
notes: Wikipedia "Visa policy of Argentina," cross-checked 2026-07-08. Venezuela gets 60 days; Jamaica/Kazakhstan/Malaysia/Macao get 30. Mercosur-associated nationals can enter on national ID card, a document-type convenience

fact_label: Tourist time counts toward Rentista/citizenship residency clock
value_raw: No
notes: Clocks only start once actual resident status is granted. Rentista can reportedly be filed for from inside Argentina after tourist entry — a filing-location convenience, not a clock credit

fact_label: Tourist time counts toward tax-residency trigger
value_raw: Yes
notes: ~183 cumulative days within a 12-month period, applies regardless of visa category — "just a tourist" the whole time can still trigger Argentine tax residency. Sits in unreconciled tension with this file's own separate "12+ months physical presence" tax-trigger language elsewhere — a named, unresolved internal discrepancy

value_raw: Yes
notes: Article 20 of the Constitution — same rights as citizens, no fideicomiso-style trust required (unlike Mexico's coastal zone), no nationwide coastal restriction. Buenos Aires city property unrestricted for foreign buyers
